title: The role of the masseter muscle in holding back words
author: Kalesh
excerpt:

metaDescription:
The jaw that holds a lifetime of unsaid things 

 I wake up some mornings and my jaw feels like it's been clamped shut for hours. The masseter muscle, tha...
htmlContent:
The jaw that holds a lifetime of unsaid things

I wake up some mornings and my jaw feels like it's been clamped shut for hours. The masseter muscle, that thick band of tissue running from your cheekbone down to your lower jaw, is rock hard. I press my fingers into it and feel the tension, the stored effort of a night spent holding something back. You probably know this feeling too. That ache in your temples. The click when you open your mouth to yawn. The dentist who tells you that you're grinding your teeth at night and suggests a mouthguard you'll never wear consistently.

Here's the thing. That muscle isn't just there for chewing. It's a keeper of secrets. It's the physical ation of every word you swallowed, every scream you silenced, every "I'm fine" you forced through a tight throat. The masseter is the body's most direct way of saying "I will not speak." And for those of us with complex trauma, it's been saying that for a very long time.

I've been thinking about this because I recently started working with a somatic therapist who pointed out that my jaw barely moves when I talk. She asked me to notice it. I did. And I felt a wave of shame, then sadness, then a kind of tired recognition. Of course my jaw is locked. Of course. What would happen if I actually let the words out? What would come pouring through that gate?

Peter Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body's unfinished responses. The deer that freezes, then shakes, then runs. The human who freezes, then stays frozen for years, decades. The masseter is a freeze response in miniature. It's the body bracing for impact, bracing for the moment when speaking becomes dangerous. And for many of us, speaking was dangerous. It still feels dangerous.

Think about it. When did you first learn to hold your tongue? Was it at the dinner table, when your parent's mood shifted and you knew any word could set them off? Was it in a classroom, when a teacher dismissed your question and you felt the heat of humiliation? Was it in a relationship, when you realized your honesty was met with punishment, withdrawal, or rage? The jaw learns. It learns fast. And it doesn't forget.

I'm not going to tell you that you just need to "release" your jaw and everything will be fine. That's the kind of wellness industry nonsense that makes me want to scream. The masseter isn't tense because you forgot to stretch. It's tense because your nervous system decided, somewhere along the way, that keeping your mouth shut was a survival strategy. And your nervous system doesn't give up survival strategies easily.

Stay with me here. I want to offer something more honest than that.

The polyvagal perspective on the locked jaw

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, gave us a framework for understanding why the body does what it does safety and threat. His work shows that our nervous system is constantly scanning for cues of safety or danger. When it detects danger, it mobilizes the body for fight or flight. When it detects life threat, it shuts down into freeze or collapse. And when it detects safety, it opens up into social engagement.

The masseter muscle is directly wired into this system. Think about what happens when you feel truly safe with someone. Your jaw relaxes. Your voice drops. You might laugh, or sigh, or speak more freely. Now think about what happens when you feel threatened. Your jaw tightens. Your teeth clench. Your voice might go high and tight, or you might stop speaking entirely.

A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical is The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link). Deb Dana, who writes extensively on applying Porges' work in clinical settings, describes how we can track these shifts in our own bodies. She calls it "glimpsing" the nervous system. You don't need to fix it. You just need to notice it. And noticing your jaw, the way it holds tension, the way it softens or hardens in response to different people and situations, is a form of glimpsing.

I've started doing this. I'll be in a conversation and I'll notice my jaw tightening. I won't try to relax it. I'll just notice. And I'll ask myself a quiet question: What just happened? What did I hear, or feel, or sense that made my body decide to lock up? Sometimes it's obvious. Sometimes it's not. But the noticing itself is a kind of intervention. It tells the nervous system that someone is paying attention. That someone is here.

Bessel van der Kolk, author of "The Body Keeps the Score," has said that trauma is not just an event that happened in the past. It's the body's ongoing response to that event. The masseter is a perfect example. The threat is over. The person who hurt you is gone, or changed, or dead. But your jaw still remembers. It still prepares for the attack that might come. It still holds the words that could get you in trouble.

Wild, right? That a muscle could hold so much history. But it does. And it will keep holding it until the nervous system gets a clear signal that it's safe to let go. Not a command. Not a stretch. A signal.

The words that got stuck in your throat

I want to talk about what happens when you actually start to let those words out. Not all at once. Not in some dramatic cathartic release. But in small, careful increments. Because the masseter doesn't in one dramatic moment. It unlocks in degrees. In millimeters.

For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering is Waking the Tiger (paid link) by Peter Levine. He describes how the body completes its unfinished responses through gentle, titrated movement and awareness. Not forcing. Not pushing. Just allowing the body to do what it was trying to do when the freeze happened.

I've been experimenting with this in my own life. I'll sit quietly and bring my attention to my jaw. I'll let it soften just a little. Then I'll imagine the words I've been holding back. Not speaking them out loud. Just imagining them. And I'll notice what my jaw does. Sometimes it tightens more. Sometimes it twitches. Sometimes it releases a tiny bit. Each response is information. None of it is wrong.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. In it, he talks about how the body's sensations are the language of trauma. The jaw tension is a sentence. The clenching is a paragraph. The grinding is a whole chapter. Learning to read that language, to listen without judgment, is the work.

I've noticed something else too. When I do let a word slip, when I say something honest about how I'm feeling, my jaw often releases afterward. Not completely. But noticeably. It's like the muscle says "okay, that one was safe. We can try another one later." And over time, those small releases add up. The jaw starts to trust that speaking won't always lead to disaster.

But here's the hard truth. Sometimes speaking does lead to disaster. Sometimes people don't want to hear what you have to say. Sometimes the old patterns are reinforced by new experiences. And the jaw tightens again. That's not failure. That's the reality of living in a body that has learned to protect itself. The masseter isn't your enemy. It's your protector. It kept you alive. It kept you quiet when quiet was the safest option. And it deserves gratitude, not frustration.

Working with the jaw without forcing it

So what do you actually do with a locked jaw? How do you work with it without making things worse? I've been exploring this question for myself, and I want to share what's been helpful. Not as a prescription. Just as a report from someone who's in the trenches with you.

First, I stopped trying to relax it. Every time I told myself to "just relax your jaw," it tightened more. The command itself felt like a demand, and my nervous system doesn't respond well to demands. Instead, I started just noticing. I'd touch my jaw gently, not to massage it, but to acknowledge it. Like putting a hand on a friend's shoulder. Just letting it know I was there.

Second, I started experimenting with small sounds. Not words. Just sounds. A hum. A sigh. A gentle "ahhh." The masseter is involved in making sound, and when I approach it through sound rather than speech, it seems more willing to cooperate. I'll hum for a few minutes and notice the vibration in my jaw. It's a way of reminding the muscle that it can move, that it can participate in something pleasant, without the pressure of meaning.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She offers exercises for tracking and shifting your nervous system state, and many of them involve gentle, non-demanding awareness of the body. It's the opposite of forcing. It's invitation.

Third, I started telling my jaw the truth. I'd say, out loud, "I know you're holding onto something. I know you're trying to protect me. Thank you. We're safe right now. You can let go a little if you want to. Or not. Either way, I'm here." This might sound strange. It might feel strange. But the body responds to acknowledgment. The masseter, that muscle that's been working so hard for so long, deserves to be thanked. It deserves to be seen.

And finally, I stopped expecting the jaw to completely. Maybe it never will. Maybe there will always be a degree of tension there, a remnant of all those years of holding back. And maybe that's okay. The goal isn't perfect relaxation. The goal is relationship. A relationship with this part of my body that has done so much for me. A relationship built on attention, respect, and patience.

Notice what just happened in your body as you read that. Did your jaw tighten? Did it release a little? Did you become aware of it for the first time in this whole article? That awareness is the beginning. Not the end. Just the beginning.

Healing Journey Resources

  • The Polyvagal Theory in Therapy (paid link) - A resource that helps make polyvagal theory practical
  • Waking the Tiger (paid link) - For those exploring somatic approaches, a book worth considering
  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing
  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
